SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear, testable claims and presents experimental comparisons to established baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on CoNLL and several in-house datasets, and reports hyperparameters; this supports the main empirical claims. However, reproducibility and robustness are weak: key resources (code, projection scripts, and in-house data) are not provided, there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance, and the annotation-selection heuristic and linear mapping deserve ablation/variance analysis to isolate contributions. Mathematical content appears minimal and adequate for the methods used. Overall the methodology is promising but missing critical experimental controls and reproducibility details, preventing a higher technical score. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by cross-lingual experiments comparing against reasonable prior baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on CoNLL and several in-house datasets, and by reporting hyperparameters and multiple architectures/co-decoding schemes. There are no formal derivations to check and the representation-mapping approach (linear embedding mapping) is standard, but the paper relies on heuristic sentence selection and in-house annotations without reported statistical significance tests, which leaves minor questions about generality and robustness. Overall the methodology is sound and the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only modest concerns about unstated assumptions and evaluation breadth.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and presents experimental comparisons to established baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on CoNLL and several in-house datasets, and reports hyperparameters; this supports the main empirical claims. However, reproducibility and robustness are weak: key resources (code, projection scripts, and in-house data) are not provided, there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance, and the annotation-selection heuristic and linear mapping deserve ablation/variance analysis to isolate contributions. Mathematical content appears minimal and adequate for the methods used. Overall the methodology is promising but missing critical experimental controls and reproducibility details, preventing a higher technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by cross-lingual experiments comparing against reasonable prior baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on CoNLL and several in-house datasets, and by reporting hyperparameters and multiple architectures/co-decoding schemes. There are no formal derivations to check and the representation-mapping approach (linear embedding mapping) is standard, but the paper relies on heuristic sentence selection and in-house annotations without reported statistical significance tests, which leaves minor questions about generality and robustness. Overall the methodology is sound and the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only modest concerns about unstated assumptions and evaluation breadth.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and the main contributions (two projection approaches, two NN architectures, and co-decoding schemes) are stated early and follow through into experiments. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough to reproduce key steps (hyperparameters are reported and related work is adequately covered), and results are presented clearly (comparisons to Täckström et al. and Tsai et al. on CoNLL and in-house datasets). Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., more explicit mention of figure/table clarity and any notation edge-cases would further strengthen readability for reproducibility-focused readers. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized with a logical progression from motivation to methods (annotation- and representation-projection, NN1/NN2 architectures, co-decoding) and experiments on CoNLL and multiple in-house datasets. The main contributions are stated early and hyperparameters/related work coverage are reported, which helps reproducibility; notation appears consistent and the writing quality is good. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective in the summary, though provenance of some in-house datasets and absence of code could have been made clearer. Overall presentation is strong with only minor clarity/practicality details that could be expanded.

Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough to reproduce key steps (hyperparameters are reported and related work is adequately covered), and results are presented clearly (comparisons to Täckström et al. and Tsai et al. on CoNLL and in-house datasets). Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., more explicit mention of figure/table clarity and any notation edge-cases would further strengthen readability for reproducibility-focused readers.

The main contributions are stated early and hyperparameters/related work coverage are reported, which helps reproducibility; notation appears consistent and the writing quality is good. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective in the summary, though provenance of some in-house datasets and absence of code could have been made clearer. Overall presentation is strong with only minor clarity/practicality details that could be expanded.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): This paper tackles an important practical problem—weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—by proposing complementary annotation- and representation-projection approaches, two neural architectures, and co-decoding schemes that empirically outperform prior work (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) and approach supervised performance for some languages. From a reproducibility-focused perspective the impact is moderate: the methods and reported gains are likely useful to researchers and practitioners seeking scalable cross-lingual NER techniques, but reliance on in-house annotated corpora for several languages and no mention of released code or public projection data limits immediate uptake and broader influence. The work opens sensible directions for combining projection and embedding-mapping methods, but given the constrained evaluation resources and absence of public artifacts its influence is useful but not field-changing. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical problem—cross-lingual NER with minimal target supervision—by combining complementary weak supervision strategies (annotation projection with a novel sentence-quality selection and representation projection via embedding mapping) and co-decoding, and it empirically outperforms established baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on multiple CoNLL languages while approaching supervised performance for some languages. From my perspective as a cross-lingual NLP researcher, the work is likely to change practitioner choices by showing that simple embedding-based representation projection can be effectively combined with projection-labeled data, and the proposed selection and co-decoding techniques are practically useful across languages. The impact is tempered by use of in-house annotated data for several languages (which may affect reproducibility) and omission of character/gazetteer features, but overall the contribution is a strong, broadly applicable advance that should be built upon in follow-up work.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—by proposing complementary annotation- and representation-projection approaches, two neural architectures, and co-decoding schemes that empirically outperform prior work (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) and approach supervised performance for some languages. From a reproducibility-focused perspective the impact is moderate: the methods and reported gains are likely useful to researchers and practitioners seeking scalable cross-lingual NER techniques, but reliance on in-house annotated corpora for several languages and no mention of released code or public projection data limits immediate uptake and broader influence. The work opens sensible directions for combining projection and embedding-mapping methods, but given the constrained evaluation resources and absence of public artifacts its influence is useful but not field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—cross-lingual NER with minimal target supervision—by combining complementary weak supervision strategies (annotation projection with a novel sentence-quality selection and representation projection via embedding mapping) and co-decoding, and it empirically outperforms established baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) on multiple CoNLL languages while approaching supervised performance for some languages. From my perspective as a cross-lingual NLP researcher, the work is likely to change practitioner choices by showing that simple embedding-based representation projection can be effectively combined with projection-labeled data, and the proposed selection and co-decoding techniques are practically useful across languages. The impact is tempered by use of in-house annotated data for several languages (which may affect reproducibility) and omission of character/gazetteer features, but overall the contribution is a strong, broadly applicable advance that should be built upon in follow-up work.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper combines two existing paradigms—annotation projection with sentence-quality heuristics and representation projection via a linear embedding mapping—into a practical hybrid system and adds simple neural taggers (NN1/NN2) plus two co-decoding schemes. These components individually are familiar from prior cross-lingual work (projection-based labeling and embedding-space mapping), so the contribution is an engineering-driven, pragmatic integration rather than a fundamentally new theoretical idea. The co-decoding strategies and the specific heuristic for selecting high-quality projected sentences are useful extensions, but overall the work reads as an incremental, moderate-novelty advance that would inform practitioners rather than surprise experts. | B(3): The paper combines several known ideas—annotation projection, linear embedding mappings to transfer lexical representations, and ensemble/co-decoding—into a practical weakly supervised NER pipeline; these components individually are not novel but their integration (especially the frequency-based sentence selection for projection and direct application of an English embedding-only NER after mapping) is a useful engineering contribution. The authors clearly position against Täckström et al. (2012) and Tsai et al. (2016) and introduce two lightweight neural architectures and co-decoding heuristics, which are non-trivial but constitute incremental advances rather than fundamentally new paradigms. An expert would find the practical choices and empirical gains informative, but the core methodological ingredients largely extend existing techniques.

The co-decoding strategies and the specific heuristic for selecting high-quality projected sentences are useful extensions, but overall the work reads as an incremental, moderate-novelty advance that would inform practitioners rather than surprise experts.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents multilingual experiments, multiple model variants (NN1/NN2), two projection approaches, and co-decoding schemes with comparisons to prior work, which supports the main claims adequately. However, the summary does not confirm thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses isolating contributions (e.g., effects of the smoothing layer, data selection heuristic, or co-decoding), code/data availability is not mentioned and many evaluation datasets are in-house, and failure analysis is limited. Related work is covered and hyperparameters are reported, but the gaps in ablations, public artifacts, and detailed reproducibility (exact preprocessing, splits, seeds, checkpoints) prevent a higher score. | B(4): The experiments are solid: the paper evaluates both annotation- and representation-projection approaches, introduces NN1/NN2 and co-decoding, and compares to prior cross-lingual baselines (Täckström 2012; Tsai 2016) on CoNLL languages plus several in-house datasets, with hyperparameters reported. However, the summary does not indicate comprehensive ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of the sentence selection heuristic, mapping quality, or each component of co-decoding) and several evaluation resources are in-house, which limits reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged (no gazetteers/char features), but failure-mode analysis appears limited. Overall, the study gives strong evidence for the claims with minor gaps in ablations and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents multilingual experiments, multiple model variants (NN1/NN2), two projection approaches, and co-decoding schemes with comparisons to prior work, which supports the main claims adequately. However, the summary does not confirm thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses isolating contributions (e.g., effects of the smoothing layer, data selection heuristic, or co-decoding), code/data availability is not mentioned and many evaluation datasets are in-house, and failure analysis is limited. Related work is covered and hyperparameters are reported, but the gaps in ablations, public artifacts, and detailed reproducibility (exact preprocessing, splits, seeds, checkpoints) prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid: the paper evaluates both annotation- and representation-projection approaches, introduces NN1/NN2 and co-decoding, and compares to prior cross-lingual baselines (Täckström 2012; Tsai 2016) on CoNLL languages plus several in-house datasets, with hyperparameters reported. However, the summary does not indicate comprehensive ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of the sentence selection heuristic, mapping quality, or each component of co-decoding) and several evaluation resources are in-house, which limits reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged (no gazetteers/char features), but failure-mode analysis appears limited. Overall, the study gives strong evidence for the claims with minor gaps in ablations and reproducibility.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses cross-lingual NER, a core NLP problem well within scope of top NLP venues and aimed appropriately at that community (uses CoNLL datasets, standard F1/precision/recall metrics, and compares against established baselines like Täckström et al. and Tsai et al.). The methodology (annotation/representation projection, neural NER variants, and co-decoding) and evaluation setup are consistent with expectations for the venue. I have reproducibility concerns—several human-annotated in-house datasets and no mention of code release are reported, which weakens empirical verifiability—so while the topic, audience, and evaluation are a good fit, these practical reproducibility gaps prevent a perfect score. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML conference topic—cross-lingual NER—with methods (annotation projection, representation/embedding mapping, neural sequence models) and evaluation (CoNLL datasets, multiple languages, precision/recall/F1) that match community expectations. The work targets the right audience by combining practical, scalable weak supervision and representation-transfer ideas relevant to cross-lingual transfer research, and it is positioned with appropriate baselines (Täckström 2012, Tsai 2016) and realistic multilingual evaluations. Given the breadth of languages, the novel combination of projection and embedding-mapping approaches, and the empirical comparisons, this paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses cross-lingual NER, a core NLP problem well within scope of top NLP venues and aimed appropriately at that community (uses CoNLL datasets, standard F1/precision/recall metrics, and compares against established baselines like Täckström et al. and Tsai et al.). The methodology (annotation/representation projection, neural NER variants, and co-decoding) and evaluation setup are consistent with expectations for the venue. I have reproducibility concerns—several human-annotated in-house datasets and no mention of code release are reported, which weakens empirical verifiability—so while the topic, audience, and evaluation are a good fit, these practical reproducibility gaps prevent a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML conference topic—cross-lingual NER—with methods (annotation projection, representation/embedding mapping, neural sequence models) and evaluation (CoNLL datasets, multiple languages, precision/recall/F1) that match community expectations. The work targets the right audience by combining practical, scalable weak supervision and representation-transfer ideas relevant to cross-lingual transfer research, and it is positioned with appropriate baselines (Täckström 2012, Tsai 2016) and realistic multilingual evaluations. Given the breadth of languages, the novel combination of projection and embedding-mapping approaches, and the empirical comparisons, this paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against reasonable and relevant prior cross-lingual NER methods (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) and uses standard CoNLL dev/test sets and F1/precision/recall metrics, which supports fair, comparable evaluation on those languages. However, several potential concerns remain for full confidence in fairness and completeness: some target evaluations use in-house annotated data (Japanese, Korean, German fine-grained, Portuguese) where baseline conditions and data splits are not fully described, and there is no mention of broader recent baselines or variability (multiple runs/statistical tests). Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions in baseline coverage and reproducibility details that prevent labeling them as fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against established cross-lingual baselines (Täckström et al. 2012; Tsai et al. 2016) and includes reasonable source-system baselines (CRF, MEMM) evaluated on standard CoNLL data, using the same metrics. However, the summary indicates some reliance on in-house datasets (Japanese, Korean, German fine-grained) where it is unclear whether baselines were run under identical conditions, and there is no mention of comparison to more recent neural cross-lingual or multilingual NER approaches that could be relevant. The authors report when they outperform the two cited prior methods but do not appear to thoroughly discuss cases where prior methods may be comparable, so comparisons are generally good but with minor omissions and limited scope.

However, several potential concerns remain for full confidence in fairness and completeness: some target evaluations use in-house annotated data (Japanese, Korean, German fine-grained, Portuguese) where baseline conditions and data splits are not fully described, and there is no mention of broader recent baselines or variability (multiple runs/statistical tests). Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions in baseline coverage and reproducibility details that prevent labeling them as fully comprehensive.

However, the summary indicates some reliance on in-house datasets (Japanese, Korean, German fine-grained) where it is unclear whether baselines were run under identical conditions, and there is no mention of comparison to more recent neural cross-lingual or multilingual NER approaches that could be relevant. The authors report when they outperform the two cited prior methods but do not appear to thoroughly discuss cases where prior methods may be comparable, so comparisons are generally good but with minor omissions and limited scope.

main_claims: Proposes two weakly supervised cross-lingual NER approaches: (1) annotation projection with a heuristic data selection scheme to pick high-quality projection-labeled sentences, and (2) representation projection that maps target-language word embeddings into English and applies a single English NER system.

Introduces two neural NER architectures (NN1 and NN2) operating on word embeddings and a smoothing layer in NN2; and two co-decoding schemes (exclude-O confidence-based and rank-based) to combine annotation- and representation-based outputs.

Shows that the combined systems outperform two prior cross-lingual NER approaches (Täckström et al., 2012; Tsai et al., 2016) on CoNLL test data and approach supervised performance for some languages.
_summary.methodology: Train English NER models using only word embeddings (NN1/NN2) and/or create projection-labeled target data via annotation projection with a frequency-based sentence quality metric; map target-language embeddings into English via a learned linear mapping to directly apply the English NER model; combine outputs using two co-decoding schemes.
datasets: In-house human-annotated NER data for Japanese
In-house human-annotated NER data for Korean
In-house human-annotated NER data for German (fine-grained >50 types)
In-house human-annotated NER data for Portuguese
limitations: The neural models do not use gazetteers or character-level representations because such features might not be readily available or might not transfer to other languages.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducibility-focused ML Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with 7+ years building and evaluating NLP systems, specializing in experimental design, ablation studies, and production-level reproducibility for sequence labeling tasks.
traits: Insists on clear, comparable baselines and ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component
Looks for statistical significance, variance reporting (e.g., multiple runs / seeds), and robust evaluation protocols
Values complete implementation details: hyperparameters, preprocessing, training schedules, and dataset splits
Checks for practical reproducibility: availability of code, data processing scripts, and exact model checkpoints
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Detailed, methodical reviewer who asks for experiments and documentation that make empirical claims verifiable and whose feedback focuses on strengthening experimental rigor and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.name: Cross-lingual NLP Senior Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor and long-time researcher in cross-lingual and multilingual NLP, with extensive publications on cross-lingual transfer, representation learning, and low-resource sequence labeling.
traits: Assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior cross-lingual NER and representation-mapping literature
Values practical impact across languages: scalability, ease of deployment, and reliance on external resources (parallel data, bilingual lexicons, embeddings)
Pays attention to broader implications: whether methods generalize to truly low-resource languages and realistic settings
Notices whether evaluation languages and datasets fairly reflect claimed improvements and whether failure modes are analyzed
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-aware reviewer who evaluates novelty, relevance, and real-world impact, emphasizing how the work advances the field and under what conditions the proposed approaches are useful.
